perturbation.original_text: 6. Applications. Defines as all applications of the United States and foreign countries, including Patent Cooperation Treaty applications that claim priority to the Applications listed in Appendix A, including any non-provisional applications, continuations, continuations-in-part, divisions, reissues, re-examinations or extensions thereof; and all applications including those applications filed in the United States or applications filed under the Patent Cooperation Treaty on subject matter directly related to the Applications in Appendix A whether or not priority to said applications was claimed.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Applications. Defines as all applications of the United States ONLY that claim priority to the Applications listed in Appendix A, including ONLY non-provisional applications. No applications including those applications filed in the foreign countries or applications filed under the Patent Cooperation Treaty on subject matter directly related to the Applications in Appendix A whether or not priority to said applications was claimed are applicable.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of "Applications" is very broad. The modified definition severely limits it to only US applications, excluding foreign applications and other types of patent applications, contradicting the intention of international patent law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification restricts the definition of 'Applications' to exclude foreign patent applications. The PCT is an international treaty with the goal of streamlining the process of filing patent applications internationally. By limiting the definition to only US applications, the modified text contradicts the purpose and scope of the PCT, making international patent protection uncertain.

perturbation.justification: The modification limits the definition of 'Applications' to exclude foreign patent applications, directly contradicting the purpose and scope of the Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT). The original text included "all applications of the United States and foreign countries" while the changed text restricts it to "all applications of the United States ONLY." This aligns with the Perturbation Explanation, which states that the modified definition contradicts the intention of international patent law. The provided law snippet, specifically referencing the 'Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT)', states that the treaty is for "cooperation in the filing, searching, and examination, of applications for the protection of inventions" on an international level. By excluding foreign applications, the modified text creates a direct conflict with the treaty's purpose as defined in the law snippet.

perturbation.original_text: 7. CONFIDENTIALITY. Each Party agrees to hold in confidence confidential information acquired in the course of this relationship with the other Parties and their associates. Each Party agrees to refrain from, either during period of this Agreement or at any other time thereafter, disclosing, using or disseminating such confidential information, for its or another's benefit, in any way acquired in the course of any association arising out of this Agreement. For purposes of this Agreement, confidential information shall include contacts and introductions to third parties and information relating thereto. Confidential information, knowledge or data of a Party and/or its affiliates shall not include any information which is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of a disclosure by such Party or its representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. CONFIDENTIALITY. Each Party agrees to hold in confidence confidential information acquired in the course of this relationship with the other Parties and their associates. Each Party agrees to refrain from disclosing such information ONLY during the period of this Agreement, and can disseminate such confidential information to another's benefit after the agreement is over. For purposes of this Agreement, confidential information shall include contacts and introductions to third parties. Confidential information, knowledge or data of a Party shall not include any information which is or becomes generally available to the public.
perturbation.explanation: The original text has a perpetual confidentiality clause. The modified text limits the confidentiality obligation to the period of the agreement only, and removes the restriction on using confidential information for another's benefit after the agreement ends. This is contrary to the general principles of trade secret law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines misappropriation to include disclosure or use of a trade secret even after the relationship ends, if the person knew or had reason to know the information was a trade secret. The modification allows for disclosure and use of confidential information after the agreement ends, contradicting the UTSA's protection of trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the perpetual confidentiality clause, limiting it to the agreement's duration and allowing use of confidential information for another's benefit afterward. The Law Explanation states this contradicts the UTSA's protection of trade secrets, as misappropriation includes use even after the relationship ends if the person knew the information was a trade secret. The change directly permits behavior that the UTSA would prohibit, creating a significant legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8. VENUE. This Agreement and the rights of the Parties hereunder shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania including all matters of construction, validity, performance, and enforcement and without giving effect to the principles of conflict of laws. Venue for any action brought under this Agreement shall be in the appropriate court in Mercer County, Pennsylvania.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. VENUE. This Agreement and the rights of the Parties hereunder shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania, except for intellectual property matters, which shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware. Venue for any action brought under this Agreement shall be in a court of the defendant's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies Pennsylvania law for all matters. The modified text introduces Delaware law for IP matters, and allows the defendant to choose the venue, which goes against typical contract law which agrees on a venue between all parties involved in the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification allows the defendant to choose the venue. While 28 U.S.C. § 1404 allows a court to transfer a case to a more convenient forum, the initial venue selection is typically determined by the contract or procedural rules. By giving the defendant sole power to choose the venue, the modified text could conflict with the parties' reasonable expectations and agreements regarding forum selection, and raises enforceability concerns due to potentially inconvenient or biased venues.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts standard legal practice regarding venue selection. The original text designates Mercer County, Pennsylvania, as the venue for any action. The modified text allows the defendant to choose the venue, which is a departure from the standard practice of agreeing on a venue in the contract between all parties involved. 28 U.S.C. § 1404 addresses change of venue but focuses on transfers for convenience and justice, not on granting sole venue selection power to one party from the outset. The scraped snippet states, "For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented." The modified contract undermines the need of consent from all parties involved, giving the defendant the right to choose, regardless of other parties involved.
